Curriculum → Foundations and estimation → Latency, throughput, and SLOs

Lesson 4 of 5

Latency, throughput, and SLOs

Reason about how much work the system can do, how long each request takes, and which percentile targets users actually feel.

25-35 min Foundations and estimation 4 guided stops

Study map

What this lesson helps you explain

This is the language that connects product promises to capacity planning, queues, caches, and admission control.

Start here Frame the trade-off before diving into architecture. 1. Latency is the user-visible delay Track p50 for typical experience and p95/p99 for worst-case experience2. Throughput is sustained work per unit time Queues can smooth bursts even when average throughput is healthy3. SLOs drive trade-offs Separate user-facing SLOs from internal targets

Guided walkthrough

Move through the lesson in a clear interview order

4 steps

Start with the framing, walk the concept in order, then use the right rail for prompts, pitfalls, and visual anchors.

1. Interview framing This is the language that connects product promises to capacity planning, queues, caches, and admission control. 2. Latency is the user-visible delay Track p50 for typical experience and p95/p99 for worst-case experience3. Throughput is sustained work per unit time Queues can smooth bursts even when average throughput is healthy4. SLOs drive trade-offs Separate user-facing SLOs from internal targets
1

Start with this framing

Interview framing

This is the language that connects product promises to capacity planning, queues, caches, and admission control.

What to say first

  • Use percentile latency, not only averages.
  • Call out both average load and peak concurrency.
  • Tie SLOs to architecture choices and fallback behavior.
2

Study section

Latency is the user-visible delay

Latency comes from network hops, serialization, locks, cache misses, storage I/O, and retries. Tail latency often dominates user pain.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Track p50 for typical experience and p95/p99 for worst-case experience
  • One slow dependency can dominate the whole request fan-out
  • Synchronous cross-region calls are expensive in latency budgets
3

Study section

Throughput is sustained work per unit time

Throughput depends on concurrency, parallelism, batching, and how much coordination each request requires.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Queues can smooth bursts even when average throughput is healthy
  • Throughput ceilings often appear before CPU is fully saturated
  • Back pressure protects latency by refusing or delaying excess work
4

Study section

SLOs drive trade-offs

A stated objective such as 99.9% of writes under 200 ms creates a design target for replicas, cache placement, and fallback behavior.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Separate user-facing SLOs from internal targets
  • Include error budgets and degraded modes
  • Make clear which endpoints need stronger guarantees than others

Checklist

Things to say clearly

  • Use percentile latency, not only averages.
  • Call out both average load and peak concurrency.
  • Tie SLOs to architecture choices and fallback behavior.
  • Discuss how you would detect SLO regression in production.

Common pitfalls

What to avoid

  • Assuming throughput improvements always help latency.
  • Ignoring tail latency in fan-out systems.
  • Treating SLOs as static even when product requirements differ by endpoint.

Interview prompts

Practice aloud

  • Why can adding retries improve availability but hurt latency?
  • How do queues change throughput without necessarily improving latency?
  • Which percentile would you optimize for a checkout flow and why?

Related topics

Keep the thread going

Availability, consistency, and CAP trade-offs Foundations and estimationQueues, streams, and background work Caching, asynchrony, and resilience

Simulation lab

Simulation lab: Latency, throughput, and SLOs

Turn estimation and baseline trade-offs into an actual workload so you can see which assumption breaks first. Apply it to latency, throughput, and slos so the trade-offs feel concrete.

Diagnostics

Things to fix or tighten

  • Line 1: Start by adding at least one heading so the answer has a visible structure.
Preview

Explain the concept

Teach "Latency, throughput, and SLOs" back to an interviewer. Summarize what it is, when you would use it, and why it matters in a real system.

Goal

Explain the topic in interviewer-friendly language before diving into implementation details.

Guardrails

  • Why can adding retries improve availability but hurt latency?
  • Latency is the user-visible delay
  • Throughput is sustained work per unit time
  • Use percentile latency, not only averages.

Suggested Structure

  • Definition
  • When to use it
  • Benefits
  • Trade-offs
